column1: 🕉️ Proto-Indo-European Myths, Tales, Fables, and Stories

column3: Associated Deities/Figures

column2: Cosmogony, origin of the world and social order
column3: Manu (First Man), Yemo (Twin), Primordial Cow, Sky Father (Dyēws Ph₂tēr)
column4: Sacrifice of the Twin by the First Man to create the world and social classes; body parts become elements (earth, sky, etc.); primordial cow provides sustenance. Parallels: Vedic (Manu & Yama), Iranian (Yima), Norse (Ymir), Roman (Remus & Romulus), Baltic (Jumis).
column5: Lincoln, Death, War, and Sacrifice; Mallory & Adams, Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture

column2: Fire hidden or born in water; hero's quest
column3: Apám Nápát, Agni, Vahagn
column4: Fire deity emerging from or residing in water; hero retrieves or reveals the fire. Parallels: Vedic (Agni), Armenian (Vahagn), Celtic (Brigit), Greek (Prometheus, Hephaestus). Symbolizes paradox and transformation.
column5: West, Indo-European Poetry and Myth; Watkins, How to Kill a Dragon

column2: Divine birth, royal legitimacy, sacred lineage
column3: Mādhāvi, Eochaid, Numitor, Sky Father
column4: Virgin birth or miraculous conception leads to the founding of royal lines. Parallels: Roman (Rhea Silvia), Irish (Macha), Indian (Devaki). Often involves divine intervention and legitimizes rulership.
column5: Dumézil, Mythes et dieux des Indo-Européens; Mallory & Adams

column2: Hero vs. serpent/dragon; restoration of order
column3: Trito (Third Hero), Vritra, Indra, Tarhunt, Zeus, Thor
column4: Hero aided by a storm god defeats a multi-headed serpent/dragon to recover stolen cattle, water, or light. Parallels: Vedic (Indra vs. Vritra), Hittite (Tarhunt vs. Illuyanka), Greek (Zeus vs. Typhon), Norse (Thor vs. Jörmungandr), Slavic (Perun vs. Veles).
column5: Watkins, How to Kill a Dragon; Lincoln, Myth, Cosmos, and Society

column2: Tragic fate, unknowingly killing one’s child
column3: Cú Chulainn, Hildebrand, Rostam, Sigurd
column4: A hero or father unknowingly kills his own son, often in battle. Parallels: Irish (Cú Chulainn & Connla), Germanic (Hildebrand & Hadubrand), Iranian (Rostam & Sohrab), Norse (Sigurd). Reflects themes of fate, identity, and tragic destiny.

column2: Quest for sacred drink, inspiration, immortality
column3: Indra, Bragi, Odin, Divine Twins, Vāsuki, Jörmungandr
column4: Sacred mead or soma as source of poetic inspiration, immortality, or divine power. Parallels: Norse (Odin’s mead), Vedic (Soma), Greek (nectar/ambrosia), Celtic (Awen). Often involves theft, contest, or creation of the drink.
column5: West; Dumézil, The Stakes of the Warrior

column2: Ritual power of weapons, divine smiths, hero’s sword
column3: Batradz, Arthur, Arjuna, Hephaestus, Wayland Smith
column4: Magical or divine weapons, often forged by supernatural smiths. Ritual gifting, burial, or inheritance of swords. Parallels: Arthur (Excalibur), Arjuna (Gandiva), Greek (Hephaestus’ weapons), Germanic (Wayland Smith).

column2: Crossing into the afterlife, river or boundary
column3: Charon, Hermóðr, Yama, Valkyries, Divine Twins
column4: The soul’s journey to the afterlife involves crossing a river or boundary, often with a psychopomp (ferryman/guide). Parallels: Greek (Charon), Norse (Hermóðr, Valkyries), Vedic (Yama, Vaitarani), Celtic (Manannán).
column5: Lincoln, Death, War, and Sacrifice; West

column2: Law, justice, and maintenance of the universe
column3: Themis, Ṛta, Arta, Mitra, Varuna
column4: Abstract principle of cosmic order, truth, and justice upheld by gods. Parallels: Vedic (Ṛta), Iranian (Asha/Arta), Greek (Themis, Dike), Roman (Fides). Central to ritual, morality, and kingship.

column3: Castor & Pollux, Aśvins, Dieva dēli, Nāsatyas
column4: Divine twins associated with horses, rescue, healing, and the dawn. Parallels: Greek (Dioskouroi), Vedic (Aśvins), Baltic (Dieva dēli), Slavic (Divji Déci). Often sons of the Sky Father, linked to fertility and protection.

Ancient Iran

column7: Simurgh (Saena) perches on the world tree; when she flies, seeds of all healing plants scatter1011.

column7: Simurgh associated with healing, wisdom, and the world tree1011.

column5: Sacred Tree: Gaokerena; Simurgh as cosmic mediator
column7: Sēnmurw as beneficent bird, guardian of the world tree and seeds10.

column7: Simurgh raises Zal atop Alburz, aids in three heroic trials, and is linked to the cosmic tree11.

column3: Nishapur, Iran
column5: Numbered Set: 7 Valleys (Search, Love, Knowledge, Detachment, Unity, Bewilderment, Poverty & Annihilation); Numbered Birds: 30 birds reach Simurgh
column7: 7 allegorical valleys as spiritual trials; 30 birds (si-murgh) reach enlightenment/unity34579.

column3: Iran, Central Asia, India
column5: Depictions of Simurgh with Sacred Tree or in heroic scenes
column7: Artistic tradition visualizes Simurgh with tree and birds, often referencing earlier myths10.

column3: Armenia, Georgia
column5: Simurgh motif often with sacred tree or as guardian
column7: Simurgh motif adopted in Christian iconography, often with tree of life10.

column3: Iran, diaspora
column5: Simurgh as symbol of unity, wisdom, healing; Sacred Tree as motif
column7: Simurgh revived as national/cultural icon; tree motif persists10.

column1: Key Numbered Sets, Trials, and Sacred Trees
Conference of the Birds - 30 birds (si-murgh) reach Simurgh at the end of the journey3459.
(Each valley represents a spiritual trial or transformation459.)
Gaokerena (Haoma Tree of Life): Simurgh perches here; when she flies, seeds of all plants scatter, symbolizing healing and fertility1011.

Tree of Knowledge / Sacred Tree of Asha: In later legends and modern retellings, Simurgh is guardian and companion of the cosmic tree, representing wisdom and the interconnectedness of all beings81011.
The Simurgh is always linked with a sacred tree (Gaokerena/Haoma/Tree of Knowledge).
The most famous numbered sets are the 7 Valleys (spiritual trials) and the 30 birds that reach Simurgh in Attar’s Conference of the Birds.
In the Shahnameh, Simurgh aids heroes in three major trials and is associated with the cosmic mountain/tree motif.
These motifs persist in art and literature across centuries and cultures.
